title: US Strikes Iran as Ceasefire Frays, NATO Divided in Ankara
subhead: Washington retaliates for tanker attacks in Hormuz with military strikes and revoked oil waivers, Iran vows 'decisive measures.' At the NATO summit in Ankara, Trump alienates allies while rewarding Turkey. Bombs target Macron in Damascus, Le Pen declares candidacy, and Sudan's El-Obeid faces siege.

sections.heading: US Strikes Iran After Tanker Attacks in Hormuz
sections.body:
US Central Command launched what it called 'powerful' strikes on Iran early Wednesday, targeting Qeshm Island, Bandar Abbas, and Sirik. Iranian state media reported injuries from shrapnel but no deaths. The strikes came in response to three separate attacks on commercial tankers in or near the Strait of Hormuz on Monday and Tuesday, including a Qatari-flagged vessel (Al-Rekayyat) and a Saudi tanker (Wadyan), both of which reported being hit by projectiles. The UK Maritime Trade Operations body confirmed one tanker reported a fire in its engine room after being struck by an unknown projectile on Monday.

Before the military response, the US Treasury revoked the oil sanctions waiver that had been part of the US-Iran memorandum signed last month — the agreement that was supposed to end the conflict. Iran's foreign ministry called the revocation proof of American 'bad faith, inconsistency, and unreliability' and said Tehran would 'take whatever measures it considers necessary to safeguard its national interests.' Iran's deputy foreign minister warned of 'decisive measures' in response to the strikes.

Qatar and Saudi Arabia both blamed Iran directly for the tanker attacks, with Qatar's foreign ministry spokesperson Majed Al Ansari holding Iran 'fully responsible.' Iran's foreign ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei pushed back, saying vessels not coordinated with Iran or tampering with tracking systems 'face a risk of collision.' The 14-point US-Iran ceasefire agreement — which includes a $300bn reconstruction fund and provisions for Oman and Iran to jointly manage Hormuz — now appears under severe strain.
sections.whyItMatters: The strikes and sanctions revocation represent the most serious breakdown of the US-Iran ceasefire framework since it was signed last month. With both sides escalating rhetoric and Iran resuming interference with commercial shipping in the world's most critical energy chokepoint, the deal that was supposed to stabilize the Gulf is now in doubt.

sections.heading: NATO Summit in Ankara: Disunity on Display
sections.body:
The NATO leaders' summit opened in Ankara on Tuesday with US President Donald Trump dominating the agenda — and not in the way the alliance hoped. Trump told reporters he only attended because the summit was hosted by 'my friend' Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, and he repeated his assertion that the US should take over Greenland, calling it territory that 'should be controlled by the United States, not by Denmark.' Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen responded that Greenland is 'not for sale' and its future is decided by its people.

Trump also lashed out at European allies over their refusal to join the US-Israel war on Iran, singling out Italy, Germany, and France for turning down his request. 'We weren't treated well because we did something in Iran,' he said, adding that he had been 'testing' whether allies would stand with the US. He even criticized UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer, despite Britain allowing US airstrikes on Iranian missile sites from British airbases.

Despite the friction, the summit produced concrete defence procurement announcements. NATO is replacing its ageing AWACS fleet with Sweden's GlobeEye aircraft, and Airbus secured a large contract for a new fleet of transport aircraft. Norway pledged 3 billion crowns ($306m) for Ukraine's air defences, specifically against ballistic missiles. Ukraine's President Zelensky, attending the summit, urged Europe to build its own anti-ballistic systems urgently, warning 'this cannot wait until 2030 or beyond.'
sections.whyItMatters: The summit exposed a deepening rift between the US and its European allies at a moment when NATO is trying to accelerate rearmament against the Russian threat. Trump's transactional framing of alliance obligations — and his simultaneous warm embrace of Turkey's autocratic leader — signals that European security dependence on Washington is becoming a vulnerability, not an asset.

sections.heading: US Lifts Sanctions on Turkey, Considers F-35 Resumption
sections.body:
In a significant policy shift, Trump announced during his Ankara meeting with Erdogan that the US will lift CAATSA sanctions on Turkey and will 'consider' resuming sales of F-35 fighter jets. Turkey was expelled from the F-35 programme in 2019 after purchasing Russian S-400 air defence systems, and CAATSA sanctions targeted its defence procurement agency with export bans and financial restrictions.

Any return to the F-35 programme would require a presidential determination that Turkey no longer possesses or operates the Russian S-400 systems — a condition not yet met. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has publicly pressured Washington against the sale, telling Fox News it would 'upset the power balance in the Middle East' guaranteed by Israeli air superiority. Republican Senator Lindsey Graham, typically a staunch Israel supporter, said he was open to the prospect and that 'a solution might be found.'
sections.whyItMatters: The move signals Trump is willing to realign US Middle East policy around personal relationships rather than established alliance frameworks. Rewarding Turkey — a NATO member that bought Russian air defences — while sanctioning Iran and criticizing European allies redraws the region's security map and could accelerate a Turkish pivot away from Western constraints.

sections.heading: Bombings Target Macron in Damascus During Landmark Visit
sections.body:
Two bomb explosions injured 18 people, including four police officers, in central Damascus during French President Emmanuel Macron's visit — the first by an EU leader to Syria since the fall of Bashar al-Assad in late 2024. The blasts occurred near the Four Seasons hotel where Macron stayed, approximately 125 metres from the building, shortly after his motorcade departed for the presidential palace. Syria's interior ministry said the devices were planted minutes before detonation, just outside the designated security perimeter for Macron's accommodation.

No group claimed responsibility, but analysts believe the French delegation was the likely target. The attack came just days after a separate bomb killed at least 10 people at a Damascus cafe near the Palace of Justice, underscoring the security challenges facing Syria's new government under President Ahmed al-Sharaa. Macron continued his visit, signing 15 bilateral agreements on civil aviation, health, and banking with Syrian counterparts. He later posted on social media: 'Nothing can smother the aspiration of Syrian women and men to live in a fully sovereign, safe, pluralistic, and united Syria.'
sections.whyItMatters: The bombings demonstrate that despite the regime change in Damascus, Syria remains dangerously unstable. Macron's visit was meant to signal Western re-engagement with post-Assad Syria and showcase a new political order — instead, it exposed how fragile security remains, and how easily extremist groups can penetrate even a heavily guarded presidential perimeter.

sections.heading: Le Pen Declares 2027 Presidential Run Despite Conviction
sections.body:
French hard-right leader Marine Le Pen announced she will run for the presidency in 2027 and appeal her embezzlement conviction to France's Court of Cassation, the country's highest civil court. The move came hours after a Paris appeal court upheld her guilty verdict for misusing €2.8m in EU funds through a fake jobs scheme, but reduced her sentence — ruling she could stand for office while wearing an electronic tag for one year. The tag order is suspended while the Court of Cassation considers the case, likely into early 2027.

Le Pen's announcement ends months of speculation that she might hand the candidacy to Jordan Bardella, the 30-year-old president of National Rally. Instead, she proposed a duo: herself as president and Bardella as prime minister. Opponents criticized the decision. Former PM Gabriel Attal noted the 'moral dimension' of running despite a conviction for embezzling public funds, while right-wing Republicans secretary-general Othman Nasrou called it 'yet another reversal that damages the French people's trust in politics.'
sections.whyItMatters: Le Pen's candidacy with a conviction — and potentially an electronic tag during the campaign — will test French voters' tolerance in a way no previous election has. If the Court of Cassation confirms her sentence before the April 2027 first round, the campaign will unfold under extraordinary circumstances that could either galvanize her anti-establishment base or finally impose a political cost.

sections.heading: Sudan's El-Obeid Under Drone Siege as Humanitarian Crisis Deepens
sections.body:
The city of El-Obeid in Sudan's North Kordofan state — a strategic hub of roughly 500,000 people — is facing an intensifying drone assault from the Rapid Support Forces (RSF), with 27 strikes recorded in June alone, the highest monthly total since the war began. The UN human rights chief, Volker Turk, confirmed at least 45 people killed and 41 injured in 15 drone strikes between 6 and 28 June. Amnesty International has warned the city could face violence on the scale of El-Fasher, where more than 6,000 people were killed in three days in what the UN described as bearing 'the hallmarks of genocide.'

The RSF has encircled El-Obeid from the north, west, and south, while the army has reinforced its positions with allied militias to maintain a vital supply corridor to eastern territories. Drone strikes have targeted fuel stations and tankers, causing severe fuel shortages and price spikes. A hospital doctor told the BBC that amputations and head injuries are common. A seven-month-old baby whose hand was amputated later died from her injuries. The city also hosts around 100,000 displaced people who fled violence elsewhere.
sections.whyItMatters: El-Obeid sits on the road between RSF-controlled western Sudan and army-held eastern territories, including the route to Khartoum. Its fall would open the path to the capital and displace hundreds of thousands more in a war that has already created the world's largest displacement crisis. The drone campaign against fuel and infrastructure is a siege tactic designed to make the city uninhabitable.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire, signed just last month to end the Hormuz conflict, is fraying fast: Washington retaliated for tanker attacks with military strikes on Iranian territory and revoked the oil sanctions waiver that was a core part of the deal. At the NATO summit in Ankara, Trump widened the rift with European allies while rewarding Turkey with a sanctions lift and a potential F-35 deal. Bombs in Damascus targeting Macron's visit exposed post-Assad Syria's fragility. Le Pen's defiant presidential candidacy under a corruption conviction sets up an extraordinary French election. And in Sudan, El-Obeid faces a drone siege that threatens to replicate the devastation of El-Fasher. Across multiple theatres, the day's developments point to the same pattern: frameworks designed to stabilize are breaking down, and the actors filling the vacuum are the ones willing to use force.
